text: The patient is positioned supine, and a preoperative anesthesia block is administered. The surgeon begins routine dissection to retract the gallbladder, using surgical instruments to manipulate and expose the area.
The surgeon retracts the gallbladder and begins mobilizing the hepatic flexure of the colon, using surgical instruments to carefully separate the colon from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects on the superior surface of the duodenum, separating the colon and continuing the dissection towards the appendix.
The surgeon has completed the separation of the hepatic flexure and ascending colon, revealing the underlying duodenum.
The surgeon carefully separates the duodenum from the lateral adhesions, revealing the anatomical structures as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon encounters significant adhesions in the area, likely due to a retroperitoneal mass, while attempting to dissect through what are typically virgin planes.

The surgeon identifies a slipped double pigtail stent within the common bile duct (CBD), initially mistaken for an artery. After denuding the duodenum, the surgeon begins the Kocherization process, starting from the superior end and progressing towards the second and third parts of the duodenum.
The surgeon retracts the duodenum medially, using an energy device to separate the lateral adhesions. As the duodenum is moved, the inferior vena cava (IVC) becomes visible.
The surgeon encounters knotty adhesions while retracting the duodenum medially. Despite the flimsy nature of the area, the adhesions on the lateral side of the duodenum are easily separable.
The surgeon exposes the second and third parts of the duodenal junction, revealing the anatomical structures and any adhesions present in this area.
In the laparoscopic view, the surgeon mobilizes the ascending colon to improve visualization and facilitate retraction. A retroperitoneal mass lesion is visible behind the third part of the duodenum.
The surgeon observes a conglomerated lymph nodal mass adjacent to the area of the aorta, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins dissection at the lateral edge to identify the inferior vena cava (IVC), which is now visible. The tissue appears densely adherent to the IVC, indicating challenging adhesions.
The surgeon identifies the medial edge of the lesion, which is densely adhered to the aorta, and decides to exclude the plan of excision in favor of taking a biopsy.
The surgeon separates fibro-fatty tissue over the retroperitoneal mass lesion using sharp scissors, taking two large tissue samples for biopsy.
The surgeon uses scissors to take a biopsy from the retroperitoneal mass lesion located behind the third part of the duodenum, avoiding tissue charring that would occur with a monopolar hook.
The surgeon places the biopsy specimen into a specimen retrieval bag after obtaining tissue samples from the lesion.

The surgeon places small biopsy pieces into a specimen retrieval bag to prevent loss within the abdominal cavity. A gauze piece is applied to the surgical site for temporary hemostatic effect, followed by the use of bipolar cautery for further hemostasis.

The surgeon carefully cauterizes the surface of the mass using bipolar cautery, having previously dissected the inferior vena cava (IVC) and aorta to understand their proximity. After securing the area, strips of absorbable gelatin sponge (ab gels) are applied to the cauterized surface.
The surgeon repositions the duodenum back to its original location, using it to apply pressure for tamponade and secure hemostasis.
